query: How to install and configure a DHCP server in RHEL 10?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

I couldn't find specific documentation for installing and configuring a DHCP server in RHEL 10. The available search results primarily refer to RHEL 5 and 6, and do not provide an updated guide for RHEL 10. It is possible that the process has changed or that a different package is used in RHEL 10.

I recommend checking the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 documentation when it becomes available or consulting Red Hat support for the most up-to-date information on DHCP server configuration in RHEL 10.

Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Quay Enterprise 3.x Tested Integrations Updated 29 Jan 2026 Tested Integrations are a defined set of specifically tested configurations and interface points between technologies that represent the most common combinations that Quay customers are using or deploying. For these integrations, Red Hat has directly, or through certified partners, exercised a full range of platform tests as part of the product release process. Issues identified as part of this testing process are highlighted in release notes for Quay Enterprise. This list of tested integrations will expand over time, however, this page is limited to only covering what Quay supported at the time the minor version GA'ed. This means that it is possible for other configurations to be "tested" and/or "supported" that are not listed. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. **How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7128989
Last updated: 2025-10-06
Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Type: Solution

Last updated: 2025-10-06
Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves performing an in-place upgrade of the operating system. Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades from one major RHEL version to the next.

Here are the general steps and considerations:

1.  **Plan the Upgrade:**
    *   Review the system requirements and limitations for the RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 upgrade.
    *   Ensure all applications, including your DHCP server configuration, are compatible with RHEL 10. You may need to consult the documentation for the specific DHCP server software you are using (e.g., ISC DHCP) to confirm compatibility or any migration steps required for its configuration or data.
    *   Back up all critical data, especially your DHCP server's configuration files and lease database.

2.  **Prepare the RHEL 9 System:**
    *   Ensure your RHEL 9 system is updated to the latest minor release.
    *   Install the `Leapp` utility and its data packages.
    *   Perform a pre-upgrade assessment using `Leapp` to identify any potential issues that might prevent a successful upgrade. Address any reported problems.

3.  **Perform the Upgrade:**
    *   Execute the `Leapp` upgrade process. This will involve a reboot.

4.  **Verify Post-Upgrade State:**
    *   After the upgrade, verify the integrity and functionality of the RHEL 10 system.
    *   Check the status of your DHCP server service and its configuration. Ensure it starts correctly and is serving IP addresses as expected.
    *   Test DHCP functionality from client machines.

For detailed instructions on the in-place upgrade process, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)

Content: As teaming is deprecated in RHEL 9 and onwards, what is a suitable replacement? Solution Verified - Updated 9 Jun 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9. Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 10. teamd libteam team2bond Issue As teaming is deprecated in RHEL 9 and onwards, what is a suitable replacement? Is there any utility to convert the network team configuration to a bond? Resolution The teamd service and the libteam library are deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and will be removed in the next major release. As a replacement, configure a bond instead of a network team. For configuring bonding from scratch, please refer to article How do I configure a bonding device on Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL)? For having a working network team configured, for example because you upgraded from an earlier RHEL version, you can migrate the configuration to a network bond that is managed by NetworkManager. The team2bond tool helps migrate teams to bonding. Kindly refer to this article for migrating a network team configuration to network bond using team2bond utility. NOTE: From RHEL 10 onwards the network teaming module is completely removed. ... Afterward, you must manually configure further settings of the bond, such as IP addresses and DNS configuration. response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. Resolution The JBoss-EAP-4.3 has reached the end of maintenance support on 31st January 2013 1 . query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

**Technology Preview of Ansible Core 2.20 in Ansible Automation Platform**
Type: Article
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/articles/7137844
Last updated: 2026-02-23
Content: As a technology preview within this release, customers are encouraged to experiment with this capability and provide feedback. Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. The paramiko connection plugin no longer supports the PARAMIKO_HOST_KEY_AUTO_ADD and PARAMIKO_LOOK_FOR_KEYS configuration keys, which were previously deprecated. Galaxy and Vault: The ansible-galaxy command no longer supports the v2 Galaxy server API. Galaxy servers hosting collections must support v3. Type: Solution

Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: What are the support options for JBoss-EAP-4.3 from February 2013 Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat JBoss Enterprise Application Platform (EAP) 4.3 Issue JBoss support states that JBoss-EAP-4.3 server release will no longer be supported from 1st February 2013 . How to get production support and updates from Red Hat ? How does Red Hat support JBoss-EAP-4.3 server from 1st February 2013 ? Resolution The JBoss-EAP-4.3 has reached the end of maintenance support on 31st January 2013 1 . Hence, it's no longer supported by Red Hat and recommended to migrate existing JBoss-EAP-4.3 application server(s) to JBoss-EAP-5.x 2 or JBoss-EAP-6.x 3 to remain supported, which ensures timely updates and bug fixes. JBoss-EAP-5.x carries JBossMessaging(JBM) as its default JMS provider with the option to switch on to HornetQ, since the release of JBoss-EAP-5.1.2 CP update . However, JBM has been deprecated and we no longer accept any Requests For Enhancements (RFEs) but for HornetQ. JBoss-EAP-6.x carries HornetQ as its default JMS provider, which offers better performance and throughput compared to the deprecated JMS broker.

query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: As a technology preview within this release, customers are encouraged to experiment with this capability and provide feedback. Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: What are the support options for JBoss-EAP-4.3 from February 2013 Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat JBoss Enterprise Application Platform (EAP) 4.3 Issue JBoss support states that JBoss-EAP-4.3 server release will no longer be supported from 1st February 2013 . How to get production support and updates from Red Hat ? How does Red Hat support JBoss-EAP-4.3 server from 1st February 2013 ? Resolution The JBoss-EAP-4.3 has reached the end of maintenance support on 31st January 2013 1 . Hence, it's no longer supported by Red Hat and recommended to migrate existing JBoss-EAP-4.3 application server(s) to JBoss-EAP-5.x 2 or JBoss-EAP-6.x 3 to remain supported, which ensures timely updates and bug fixes. JBoss-EAP-5.x carries JBossMessaging(JBM) as its default JMS provider with the option to switch on to HornetQ, since the release of JBoss-EAP-5.1.2 CP update . However, JBM has been deprecated and we no longer accept any Requests For Enhancements (RFEs) but for HornetQ. JBoss-EAP-6.x carries HornetQ as its default JMS provider, which offers better performance and throughput compared to the deprecated JMS broker.

query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: As a technology preview within this release, customers are encouraged to experiment with this capability and provide feedback. Module and Plugin Improvements Ansible Core 2.20 includes a number of practical improvements to built-in modules and plugins: blockinfile and lineinfile module — A new encoding option has been added, enabling support for files in encodings other than UTF-8. This is a welcome improvement for customers managing legacy systems or applications that use region-specific character encodings. stat module — SELinux context is now available as a return value, with a new option to control this return. This improves visibility into the security context of files for customers in SELinux-enforcing environments. setup module (facts) — Added a new lvs subkey within each entry of ansible_facts['vgs'] to provide complete logical volume data scoped by volume group. The top-level lvs fact deduplicates logical volume names across volume groups and may be incomplete, making this new per-VG view essential for environments with complex LVM configurations. default callback plugin — A new option allows you to configure indentation for JSON and YAML output, giving you control over how task output is formatted for readability. deb822_repository module — Automatic installation of the python3-debian package when missing, reducing prerequisites for managing APT repositories on Debian-based target nodes. Removal of Deprecated Functionality Ansible Core 2.20 completes the removal of functionality that was deprecated in prior releases. Customers who have been tracking deprecation warnings in Ansible Core 2.19 should review the following changes carefully: Transport Changes: The DEFAULT_TRANSPORT configuration option no longer supports the smart value. This was previously deprecated in Ansible Core 2.16. The smart value historically selected between ssh and paramiko based on platform configuration. Customers should explicitly set their transport to ssh if not already doing so. Type: Solution

Last updated: 2025-10-06
Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: What are the support options for JBoss-EAP-4.3 from February 2013 Solution Verified - Updated 6 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat JBoss Enterprise Application Platform (EAP) 4.3 Issue JBoss support states that JBoss-EAP-4.3 server release will no longer be supported from 1st February 2013 . How to get production support and updates from Red Hat ? How does Red Hat support JBoss-EAP-4.3 server from 1st February 2013 ? Resolution The JBoss-EAP-4.3 has reached the end of maintenance support on 31st January 2013 1 . Hence, it's no longer supported by Red Hat and recommended to migrate existing JBoss-EAP-4.3 application server(s) to JBoss-EAP-5.x 2 or JBoss-EAP-6.x 3 to remain supported, which ensures timely updates and bug fixes. JBoss-EAP-5.x carries JBossMessaging(JBM) as its default JMS provider with the option to switch on to HornetQ, since the release of JBoss-EAP-5.1.2 CP update . However, JBM has been deprecated and we no longer accept any Requests For Enhancements (RFEs) but for HornetQ. JBoss-EAP-6.x carries HornetQ as its default JMS provider, which offers better performance and throughput compared to the deprecated JMS broker.

query: What DHCP server options are available in RHEL 10?
response:
The ISC DHCP server is available in Red Hat Enterprise Linux. While specific documentation for RHEL 10 DHCP server options was not found in the search, the general configuration for DHCP servers on RHEL typically involves the `dhcp` package and its configuration file, `/etc/dhcpd.conf`.

To configure a DHCP server, you would typically:
1.  Install the `dhcp` package.
2.  Edit the `/etc/dhcpd.conf` file to define subnets, ranges, and other DHCP options. A sample configuration file is usually located at `/usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample`.
3.  Start and enable the `dhcpd` service.

For precise and up-to-date information on DHCP server options and configuration in RHEL 10, it's recommended to consult the official Red Hat Enterprise Linux documentation for RHEL 9 or the latest available version, as features and best practices can evolve between releases.

**Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6971326
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Deprecated JVM tuning options might affect broker migration from Java 8 to Java 11 and later Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat AMQ 7.9 and later Issue The Artemis tuning guide [1] no longer recommends deprecated JVM tuning options like -XX:+AggressiveOpts and -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods although earlier versions (e.g., [2]) did. There are doubtless AMQ 7 installations in the field that are making use of these options. Although the removal of these options from the JVM command line may be sufficient to restore Java 11 compatibility, it isn't clear what effect this change has on broker tuning. [1] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/latest/perf-tuning.html [2] Content from activemq.apache.org is not included. https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/documentation/2.1.0/perf-tuning.html Resolution In most cases Red Hat would not expect the removal of the deprecated tuning options to have any effect on broker performance. However, we have had reports of reduced broker performance when migrating from Java 8 to Java 11, when the only change made is to remove the deprecated options. There is no Java 11 equivalent of -XX:+UseFastAccessorMethods because it is considered to be broken. However, the effect of -XX:+AggressiveOpts can be restored using the following switches: -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=20000 -XX:BiasedLockingStartupDelay=500 If you notice a reduction in broker performance when moving from Java 8 to Java 11, and nothing else is different, it is probably worthwhile to try these equivalent settings. ... In most cases we would expect this to lead to a better overall throughput -- and G1 is now the preferred GC for all supported JVMs -- but, if it does not, the Java 8 behaviour can be restored using +XX:+UseParallelGC . Root Cause -XX:+AggressiveOpts was widely recommended for many Java server-side applications, even though its implications were not well-understood. It is deprecated in Java 11, and removed in Java 12, because administrators are encouraged to use specific tuning options, that allow fine-grained control over JVM behaviour. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions. The default configuration directory location /etc/dirsrv/slapd- is now hard-coded and thus is no longer compatible with the config_dir parameter customization. Resolution The JBoss-EAP-4.3 has reached the end of maintenance support on 31st January 2013 1 . Hence, it's no longer supported by Red Hat and recommended to migrate existing JBoss-EAP-4.3 application server(s) to JBoss-EAP-5.x 2 or JBoss-EAP-6.x 3 to remain supported, which ensures timely updates and bug fixes. JBoss-EAP-6.x carries HornetQ as its default JMS provider, which offers better performance and throughput compared to the deprecated JMS broker.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059

Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released).
