**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

query: What changed in Python between RHEL 9 and RHEL 10?
response:
Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 (RHEL 10) provides Python 3.12 as a Full Life Application Stream, with a release date of May 2025.

In contrast, RHEL 9 offers multiple Python 3 Application Streams:

*   Python 3.10: Released May 2022, retirement date November 2024.
*   Python 3.11: Released November 2023, retirement date May 2026.

This means that RHEL 10 ships with a newer major version of Python (3.12) compared to the default or latest available Python 3 in RHEL 9.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. **Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Why does Leapp upgrade not prevent upgrades from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Solution Unverified - Updated 24 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Pacemaker cluster Filesystem resource agents using fstype=gfs2 Issue Why does leapp upgrade not prevented when upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 when there are gfs2 Filesystems configured? Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. The issue is that the leapp upgrade should either not continue with upgrade or provide some type of warning to the end user when it finds unsupported resources configured for pacemaker . The resources controld , Filesystems with fstype=gfs2 , or lvmlockd are no longer supported on RHEL 10. For more information see: Support Policies for RHEL Resilient Storage - RHEL High Availability Requirements for Resilient Storage Resilient Storage Add-On will be discontinued with RHEL 10 NOTE: The leapp upgrade should be successful, but once pacemaker is started on the RHEL 10 cluster nodes those resource-agents that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 will fail to start. Content: As the platform moves toward a container-first model, the RPM-based installer is being deprecated in the Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 release and removed in a future release. In future releases, customers will have the option to use the operator if they are OpenShift users or the containerized installer that supports deployments on RHEL. Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. So the basic methodology to determine if a specific PCI card is recognized by a specific kernel version is a two step process: Method 1: Determine if driver has already been assigned Step 1: Use lspci -nnnvk and check card's output block for a driver: being present See " How to determine the make and model of HBA and version of associated driver from a RHEL Server? ... **Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy**
Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Type: Solution

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Solution

Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. **Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. **Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy**
Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

**Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). Scope of Coverage Description Support Time period (in years) after general availability 10 Unlimited-incident technical support 1 Yes Asynchronous security errata 2, 4 Yes Asynchronous bug-fix errata 3, 4 Yes Software enhancements Yes, during years 0-3 1 Details of the support services are provided in the subscription agreement.. 2 See the Issue Severity Classification page for a description of Red Hat’s security severity classification. 3 Red Hat may address catastrophic issues with significant business impact for the customer through a hotfix as a temporary measure while the bug-fix errata is being created. 4 All errata provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis.

All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

I was unable to find specific Red Hat documentation detailing the `rpm-ostree install` command within the provided search results.

Content: How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database Solution Unverified - Updated 6 Feb 2025 Environment You use libdb on RHEL 8 or RHEL 9 and want to upgrade to RHEL 10, where libdb is no longer available. Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

The KDE Desktop packages are not found in RHEL 7.6 and above releases. Resolution As of RHEL 7.6 , KDE is no longer being offered. Quoting the release notes : KDE has been deprecatedKDE Plasma Workspaces (KDE), which has been provided as an alternative to the default GNOME desktop environment has been deprecated. Note : This doesn't imply that all the RHEL 7 KDE packages (release in 7.5 and older releases) would now be unsupported. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

I was unable to find specific Red Hat documentation detailing the `rpm-ostree install` command within the provided search results.

Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

I was unable to find specific Red Hat documentation detailing the `rpm-ostree install` command within the provided search results.

Issue The libdb package was deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) 9 and has been removed from RHEL 10. Users of libdb who want to upgrade to RHEL 10 must migrate to a different key-value database before switching to RHEL 10. Resolution RHEL 9.5 provides libdb 5.3.28 . The libdb-utils package provides a utility for conversion to GDBM database format. On the RHEL 9 host: Ensure whether migration is needed. Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

I was unable to find specific Red Hat documentation detailing the `rpm-ostree install` command within the provided search results.

Check your binaries and search for a libdb requirement, for example: $ find /usr/bin /usr/local/bin -type f -executable -exec ldd {} + 2>/dev/null | grep libdb Locate your database’s files. ... For a list of possible replacements, see the Red Hat Knowledgebase solution Available replacements for the deprecated Berkeley DB (libdb) . In the case of GDBM, you can use the db_converter utility included in the libdb-utils package. To install the package, enter: $ dnf install libdb-utils Backup your database. Run db_converter, for example: $ db_converter --src access.db --dest access.gdbm Adapt your application for the GDBM database backend. Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. Content: Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 Upcoming Changes Updated 23 Oct 2024 This article provides information about the upcoming changes, and deprecated, removed, and changed features in the upcoming release of Red Hat Ansible Automation Platform 2.5. For the latest updates, see the Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 Release Notes . Upcoming changes Installation changes Starting with Ansible Automation Platform 2.5, three different on-premise deployment models are fully tested. In addition to the existing RPM-based installer and operator, support for the containerized installer is being added. As the platform moves toward a container-first model, the RPM-based installer is being deprecated in the Ansible Automation Platform 2.5 release and removed in a future release. In future releases, customers will have the option to use the operator if they are OpenShift users or the containerized installer that supports deployments on RHEL. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

I was unable to find specific Red Hat documentation detailing the `rpm-ostree install` command within the provided search results.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

Here's the general process:

1.  **Install the package:**
    ```bash
    rpm-ostree install <package-name>
    ```
    Replace `<package-name>` with the actual name of the package you want to install.

2.  **Reboot the system:** For the changes to take effect, a reboot is usually required.
    ```bash
    systemctl reboot
    ```

After the reboot, the layered package will be available on your system.

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` systems are designed to be immutable, and layering packages should be done judiciously. For applications, containerization (e.g., using Podman) is often the preferred method on `rpm-ostree` based systems.

**Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability**
Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL

Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability Overview Starting with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, software is delivered through a mechanism Red Hat brands Application Streams, or AppStreams for short. AppStreams enable the flexible delivery of multiple versions of software during a major release of RHEL, decoupling the respective Appstream’s content; whether RPMs or Modules from the overarching life cycle, enabling select userland content to be refreshed on a more regular cadence. The Container Tools encompass software packages required to build and run Linux Containers on Red Hat Enterprise Linux. The primary piece of software core to this set is Podman, but there are many other pieces of software including, but not limited to Buildah and Skopeo. Released via an AppStream, the content within the Container Tools can be available and released more quickly than pieces of software in core RHEL. The objective of this document is to outline the content and support levels made available through the various Container Tools AppStreams, ensuring customers and partners can effectively plan for and deploy to their systems. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, Container Tools are provided as RPM packages via a single, rolling Application Stream. ... These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo, and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. Extended Update Support (EUS) In RHEL 9 and RHEL 10, the Container Tools AppStream provides support and maintenance updates for the available Extended Update Support (EUS) channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS or Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 minor release. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Container Tools are delivered through two different categories of Application Stream Modules ; a single rolling stream which is supported throughout the entire life of RHEL 8; and several stable streams which are released during the active phase of RHEL 8. Each of these are differentiated by the rate of change and maintenance for the content. The Rolling Stream enables developers to get access to the latest version of Container Tools Module content, delivered up to four times per year. These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. As and when required, packages may be added to the module per the Container Tools product roadmap. This Stream spans the entire RHEL 8 life cycle, and is continually updated throughout the RHEL Full Support phase to provide the latest cutting edge features for the Container Tools Module. The Stable Streams help users manage risk. This is a traditional value proposition for Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and the container-tools module continues to deliver on this. Users who just want to install, can “lock in” on a stable version, in lieu of the introduction of new features. ... Previously, the rolling stream provided neither support nor maintenance updates in conjunction with these channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 8 minor release. After this point security fixes will be backported for the life of the EUS release. Select bug fixes may be made available at Red Hat’s discretion. For more information about Extended Update Support please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability Overview Starting with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, software is delivered through a mechanism Red Hat brands Application Streams, or AppStreams for short. AppStreams enable the flexible delivery of multiple versions of software during a major release of RHEL, decoupling the respective Appstream’s content; whether RPMs or Modules from the overarching life cycle, enabling select userland content to be refreshed on a more regular cadence. The Container Tools encompass software packages required to build and run Linux Containers on Red Hat Enterprise Linux. The primary piece of software core to this set is Podman, but there are many other pieces of software including, but not limited to Buildah and Skopeo. Released via an AppStream, the content within the Container Tools can be available and released more quickly than pieces of software in core RHEL. The objective of this document is to outline the content and support levels made available through the various Container Tools AppStreams, ensuring customers and partners can effectively plan for and deploy to their systems. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, Container Tools are provided as RPM packages via a single, rolling Application Stream. ... These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo, and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. Extended Update Support (EUS) In RHEL 9 and RHEL 10, the Container Tools AppStream provides support and maintenance updates for the available Extended Update Support (EUS) channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS or Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 minor release. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Container Tools are delivered through two different categories of Application Stream Modules ; a single rolling stream which is supported throughout the entire life of RHEL 8; and several stable streams which are released during the active phase of RHEL 8. Each of these are differentiated by the rate of change and maintenance for the content. The Rolling Stream enables developers to get access to the latest version of Container Tools Module content, delivered up to four times per year. These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. As and when required, packages may be added to the module per the Container Tools product roadmap. This Stream spans the entire RHEL 8 life cycle, and is continually updated throughout the RHEL Full Support phase to provide the latest cutting edge features for the Container Tools Module. The Stable Streams help users manage risk. This is a traditional value proposition for Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and the container-tools module continues to deliver on this. Users who just want to install, can “lock in” on a stable version, in lieu of the introduction of new features. ... Previously, the rolling stream provided neither support nor maintenance updates in conjunction with these channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 8 minor release. After this point security fixes will be backported for the life of the EUS release. Select bug fixes may be made available at Red Hat’s discretion. For more information about Extended Update Support please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . Every PCI card has a header defined by the PCI standard that is read by the kernel. Included within that standard on-card header is a V endor ID (VID) and D evice ID (DID). All previous versions of software packages and linked erratas will remain available exclusively to customers with an appropriate RHEL subscription, per the governing RHEL life cycle policy. **Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability**
Type: Documentation

Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability Overview Starting with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, software is delivered through a mechanism Red Hat brands Application Streams, or AppStreams for short. AppStreams enable the flexible delivery of multiple versions of software during a major release of RHEL, decoupling the respective Appstream’s content; whether RPMs or Modules from the overarching life cycle, enabling select userland content to be refreshed on a more regular cadence. The Container Tools encompass software packages required to build and run Linux Containers on Red Hat Enterprise Linux. The primary piece of software core to this set is Podman, but there are many other pieces of software including, but not limited to Buildah and Skopeo. Released via an AppStream, the content within the Container Tools can be available and released more quickly than pieces of software in core RHEL. The objective of this document is to outline the content and support levels made available through the various Container Tools AppStreams, ensuring customers and partners can effectively plan for and deploy to their systems. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, Container Tools are provided as RPM packages via a single, rolling Application Stream. ... These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo, and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. Extended Update Support (EUS) In RHEL 9 and RHEL 10, the Container Tools AppStream provides support and maintenance updates for the available Extended Update Support (EUS) channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS or Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 minor release. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Container Tools are delivered through two different categories of Application Stream Modules ; a single rolling stream which is supported throughout the entire life of RHEL 8; and several stable streams which are released during the active phase of RHEL 8. Each of these are differentiated by the rate of change and maintenance for the content. The Rolling Stream enables developers to get access to the latest version of Container Tools Module content, delivered up to four times per year. These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. As and when required, packages may be added to the module per the Container Tools product roadmap. This Stream spans the entire RHEL 8 life cycle, and is continually updated throughout the RHEL Full Support phase to provide the latest cutting edge features for the Container Tools Module. The Stable Streams help users manage risk. This is a traditional value proposition for Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and the container-tools module continues to deliver on this. Users who just want to install, can “lock in” on a stable version, in lieu of the introduction of new features. ... Previously, the rolling stream provided neither support nor maintenance updates in conjunction with these channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 8 minor release. After this point security fixes will be backported for the life of the EUS release. Select bug fixes may be made available at Red Hat’s discretion. For more information about Extended Update Support please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . **Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability**
Type: Documentation

Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability Overview Starting with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, software is delivered through a mechanism Red Hat brands Application Streams, or AppStreams for short. AppStreams enable the flexible delivery of multiple versions of software during a major release of RHEL, decoupling the respective Appstream’s content; whether RPMs or Modules from the overarching life cycle, enabling select userland content to be refreshed on a more regular cadence. The Container Tools encompass software packages required to build and run Linux Containers on Red Hat Enterprise Linux. The primary piece of software core to this set is Podman, but there are many other pieces of software including, but not limited to Buildah and Skopeo. Released via an AppStream, the content within the Container Tools can be available and released more quickly than pieces of software in core RHEL. The objective of this document is to outline the content and support levels made available through the various Container Tools AppStreams, ensuring customers and partners can effectively plan for and deploy to their systems. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, Container Tools are provided as RPM packages via a single, rolling Application Stream. ... These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo, and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. Extended Update Support (EUS) In RHEL 9 and RHEL 10, the Container Tools AppStream provides support and maintenance updates for the available Extended Update Support (EUS) channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS or Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 minor release. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Container Tools are delivered through two different categories of Application Stream Modules ; a single rolling stream which is supported throughout the entire life of RHEL 8; and several stable streams which are released during the active phase of RHEL 8. Each of these are differentiated by the rate of change and maintenance for the content. The Rolling Stream enables developers to get access to the latest version of Container Tools Module content, delivered up to four times per year. These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. As and when required, packages may be added to the module per the Container Tools product roadmap. This Stream spans the entire RHEL 8 life cycle, and is continually updated throughout the RHEL Full Support phase to provide the latest cutting edge features for the Container Tools Module. The Stable Streams help users manage risk. This is a traditional value proposition for Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and the container-tools module continues to deliver on this. Users who just want to install, can “lock in” on a stable version, in lieu of the introduction of new features. ... Previously, the rolling stream provided neither support nor maintenance updates in conjunction with these channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 8 minor release. After this point security fixes will be backported for the life of the EUS release. Select bug fixes may be made available at Red Hat’s discretion. For more information about Extended Update Support please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle . Content: Details Red Hat UBI is designed to provide a minimal footprint of user space packages for the building and distribution of applications as container images. Red Hat UBIs include select software components from major versions of Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) and Red Hat Software Collections (SCL). Per the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle, Red Hat makes commercially reasonable efforts to ensure consistency for the available software set across all minor releases, in the event that exceptions are required, notices for software deprecation will be provided as errata. Software changes to Red Hat UBI are delivered to active RHEL subscribers through individual updates known as errata advisories. ... Red Hat will make available only the current versions of software packages required for UBI, including their dependencies on the public CDN. All previous versions of software packages and linked erratas will remain available exclusively to customers with an appropriate RHEL subscription, per the governing RHEL life cycle policy. Red Hat UBI container images are made available by Red Hat on RHCC, images which have been superseded by a newer release will remain accessible throughout the full and maintenance support phases of the respective RHEL major release. In order to ensure Red Hat UBI container images are pre-built with the latest errata, Red Hat aims to re-build and distribute available Red Hat UBI images on a 6 weekly cadence, or sooner if triggered by the release of a CVE rated as Critical or Important . ... Updates will only be provided for the most current container images and packages. Life Cycle Phases The content availability for each Red Hat UBI release is governed by the life cycle of the respective RHEL release. For instance, the Universal Base Image version 7 will follow the life cycle phases and dates outlined by the RHEL7 life cycle, with technical support only being available to Red Hat customers with appropriate subscriptions and using UBI on a RH supported stack, outlined in the container support policy. Red Hat provides no assurances of Red Hat UBI availability, life cycle or updates outside of the RHEL life cycle terms for active RHEL subscribers. NOTE: There is no EUS life cycle phase with UBI. EUS is available to customers only as part of a RHEL Premium subscription. Category: Supportability

Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Container Tools AppStream - Content Availability Overview Starting with Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, software is delivered through a mechanism Red Hat brands Application Streams, or AppStreams for short. AppStreams enable the flexible delivery of multiple versions of software during a major release of RHEL, decoupling the respective Appstream’s content; whether RPMs or Modules from the overarching life cycle, enabling select userland content to be refreshed on a more regular cadence. The Container Tools encompass software packages required to build and run Linux Containers on Red Hat Enterprise Linux. The primary piece of software core to this set is Podman, but there are many other pieces of software including, but not limited to Buildah and Skopeo. Released via an AppStream, the content within the Container Tools can be available and released more quickly than pieces of software in core RHEL. The objective of this document is to outline the content and support levels made available through the various Container Tools AppStreams, ensuring customers and partners can effectively plan for and deploy to their systems. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, Container Tools are provided as RPM packages via a single, rolling Application Stream. ... These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo, and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. Extended Update Support (EUS) In RHEL 9 and RHEL 10, the Container Tools AppStream provides support and maintenance updates for the available Extended Update Support (EUS) channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 EUS or Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 minor release. ... Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 In Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8, Container Tools are delivered through two different categories of Application Stream Modules ; a single rolling stream which is supported throughout the entire life of RHEL 8; and several stable streams which are released during the active phase of RHEL 8. Each of these are differentiated by the rate of change and maintenance for the content. The Rolling Stream enables developers to get access to the latest version of Container Tools Module content, delivered up to four times per year. These content releases will continually rebase on the latest, stable, upstream versions of Podman, Buildah, Skopeo and other tools like CRIU and Udica. Since these are always new versions, they will include new features, bug fixes, and security updates. As and when required, packages may be added to the module per the Container Tools product roadmap. This Stream spans the entire RHEL 8 life cycle, and is continually updated throughout the RHEL Full Support phase to provide the latest cutting edge features for the Container Tools Module. The Stable Streams help users manage risk. This is a traditional value proposition for Red Hat Enterprise Linux, and the container-tools module continues to deliver on this. Users who just want to install, can “lock in” on a stable version, in lieu of the introduction of new features. ... Previously, the rolling stream provided neither support nor maintenance updates in conjunction with these channels. Systems configured to use Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 EUS will receive updates to the Container Tools content through the life of each RHEL EUS release. The version of Podman, Buildah, and Skopeo will remain the same for the first 3 months of the active RHEL 8 minor release. After this point security fixes will be backported for the life of the EUS release. Select bug fixes may be made available at Red Hat’s discretion. For more information about Extended Update Support please see the Red Hat Enterprise Linux Life Cycle .
